claim: Synology reversed its 2025 third-party drive ban with the DSM 7.3 update in October 2025.
quote: third-party HDD and SSD support returns with DiskStation Manager 7.3 update

claim: Synology's April 2025 policy required Synology-branded drives or HCL-listed third-party drives on the 2025 Plus series, blocking storage pool creation otherwise.
quote: all Plus series models released from 2025 onward will require either Synology's own hard drives or third-party hard drives certified by Synology

claim: DSM 7.3 restored third-party HDD and 2.5" SATA SSD support on 2025 Plus/Value/J series, but M.2 SSDs remain HCL-locked and 2026 model behaviour is uncertain.
quote: Synology has not committed to enabling 3rd party HDD support on its 2026 devices

claim: The reversal followed reports of poor sales and substantial customer backlash on the affected 2025 models.
quote: Synology backtracks on 3rd-party HDD ban as NAS sales plummet

claim: Long-time DSM users describe Synology's interest in home consumers as at an all-time low and have begun planning exits regardless of the 2025 reversal.
quote: Synology's interest in home consumers is at an all-time low

claim: TrueNAS recommends Syncthing or rclone-mediated migration from third-party NAS appliances; SMB-mounted source preserves metadata.
quote: Users migrating data from an existing third-party NAS solution to TrueNAS 24.04 (Dragonfish) can do so using the Syncthing Enterprise application to mount the source with a remote SMB share

claim: Synology drives use mdadm + LVM + ext4/btrfs and cannot be reused directly under TrueNAS ZFS — temporary staging storage is required.
quote: Synology uses mdadm raid with LVM and ext4, while TrueNAS uses ZFS

claim: SHR is implemented as stacked mdadm devices aggregated by LVM and can be assembled read-only on a Linux host.
quote: SHR is effectively a series of mdadm devices aggregated by LVM

claim: Mount procedure for an SHR1 disk on Linux is `mdadm --assemble --run` on the data partition, then mount the LVM logical volume.
quote: sudo mdadm --assemble --run /dev/md0 /dev/sdb5 ... sudo mount /dev/mapper/vg1000-lv /mnt/synology

claim: A homelab user moved 3 TB from an SHR Synology to Unraid via rsync at ~140 MB/s on 12-year-old hardware.
quote: My throughput was on average 140MB/sec which is not too bad for all the virtual driver stacks it needs to traverse on 12 year old hardware

claim: Mounting the Synology SMB share directly from TrueNAS and rsyncing locally is the fastest two-machine transfer path.
quote: SMB-based file transfer using Synology's File Station tool has proven fast and efficient

claim: TrueNAS 25.04 "Fangtooth" (April 2025) unifies CORE and SCALE codebases and ships OpenZFS 2.3 with RAIDZ vdev expansion.
quote: supercharged RAIDZ Expansion feature allows expanding pools one drive at a time

claim: OpenZFS 2.3 enables single-drive RAIDZ expansion, removing a long-standing reason home users avoided ZFS.
quote: OpenZFS 2.3 is here, with RAID expansion and faster dedup

claim: TrueNAS leads on data integrity (ZFS), Unraid leads on flexibility, OMV leads on simplicity and minimal hardware demands.
quote: TrueNAS is recommended for data integrity, Unraid for flexibility, and OpenMediaVault for simplicity

claim: Unraid remains a paid product (license fee) while TrueNAS and OpenMediaVault are free; Unraid's app store is the most beginner-friendly.
quote: Unlike TrueNAS and OpenMediaVault, Unraid is a paid product

claim: A homelab user ran TrueNAS SCALE for over a year then moved containers/VMs to Proxmox, keeping TrueNAS as a storage VM.
quote: TrueNAS SCALE tried to do everything, so I gave it one job and moved the rest to Proxmox

claim: Proxmox-on-bare-metal with TrueNAS as a VM is a popular pattern: hypervisor flexibility plus ZFS storage.
quote: you can run TrueNAS as a VM, and in experience, TrueNAS has run extremely well as a Proxmox VM

claim: Hands-on review concludes moving from DSM to TrueNAS is a clear win for users prioritising data integrity and hardware freedom.
quote: Moving from my NAS from DSM to TrueNAS is the best storage decision I ever made

claim: TerraMaster, QNAP, UGREEN, Asustor and DIY (TrueNAS/Unraid) are the practical replacement targets cited during the April 2025 controversy.
quote: Synology Just Locked Down Your NAS Drives Here's What to Use Instead

claim: UGREEN's high-end DXP NAS units pair Intel/AMD CPUs and Docker support, putting price/performance pressure on DSM.
quote: I tested Ugreen's most powerful NAS, and it exposes everything Synology is getting wrong

claim: UGOS Pro reached daily-driver stability in 2026 but its app library (~25 first-party) is dwarfed by DSM's; Docker is the workaround.
quote: UGOS Pro is now stable enough for daily driver use ... The app library remains limited (~25 apps vs Synology's 200+)

claim: Immich and PhotoPrism are the two leading self-hosted Synology Photos replacements; Immich's mobile UX is closest to DSM Photos.
quote: PhotoPrism and Immich are two of the very best Synology Photos alternatives

claim: Immich is an actively-developed Google Photos / Synology Photos replacement with mobile apps and on-device ML.
quote: self-hosted photo and video backup solution that lets you upload photos and videos from your browser or mobile phone via dedicated Android and iOS apps

claim: PhotoPrism is the metadata-rich power-user alternative — heavier setup, deeper EXIF handling than Immich.
quote: privately hosted app for browsing, organizing, and sharing your photo collection

claim: Synology Drive only covers file sync; replacing the broader DSM bundle requires Nextcloud (full-featured) or Seafile (lean sync).
quote: Synology Drive covers file sync but relies on separate Synology packages for calendar and photos

claim: Seafile uses block-level sync and outperforms Nextcloud on raw sync speed; recommended when you only need Drive replacement.
quote: Seafile using a block-based sync approach, which essentially only syncs blocks of files that have undergone some change

claim: Frigate, Scrypted, Shinobi, ZoneMinder and Viseron are the practical Surveillance Station replacements; Frigate leads on AI-assisted detection.
quote: Other great apps like Synology Surveillance Station are ZoneMinder, Viseron, Shinobi and motionEye

claim: Frigate is an open-source NVR with on-device object detection via Coral/GPU acceleration.
quote: open-source software NVR that offers out-of-the-box AI-assisted object detection

claim: Active Backup for Business does not natively back up Proxmox VMs/containers — only file-level via the agent inside a host.
quote: Active Backup for Business does not directly support backing up Proxmox VMs

claim: Proxmox Backup Server is the recommended replacement for Active Backup for Business in a Proxmox-centric homelab.
quote: Proxmox Backup Server is recommended for running on the same Proxmox host or on a separate machine

claim: Hyper Backup can target TrueNAS via rsync over SSH, allowing a phased migration where DSM continues writing while you stand up the new system.
quote: How To - Use TrueNAS as rsync Backup Destination for Synology Hyper Backup

claim: Most DSM "apps" already run as containers under the hood — moving to Docker on a new OS is largely a docker-compose authoring exercise.
quote: Docker made migration to a new NAS easy

claim: OpenMediaVault is a Debian-based NAS OS — lightest hardware footprint and the closest UX match to DSM for non-ZFS users.
quote: OpenMediaVault is built directly on top of Debian Linux

claim: Unraid migration thread documents the standard approach: prep array on new hardware, rsync over SMB, point clients at new shares.
quote: Migrating from Synology DSM to Unraid

claim: DSM 8 beta was targeted for late 2025 but has slipped; full release expected in 2026, leaving 7.3.x as the active branch.
quote: DSM 8 is on the horizon, with a beta release planned for Q4 2025 and a full rollout in 2026
